data.title: Trump Declines to Rule Out Recession — WSJ
data.description:
By Tim Hanrahan

WASHINGTON — President Trump declined to rule out that the U.S. economy would contract this year and enter a recession, saying that his sweeping economic agenda could cause short-term turbulence that he believed would drive future prosperity.

Asked in a "Sunday Morning Futures" interview on Fox News if he was expecting a recession this year, Trump replied: "I hate to predict things like that. There is a period of transition because what we're doing is very big."

Trump has moved to overhaul the U.S. economy in an agenda focusing on new tariffs, limiting immigration and cutting regulations, government jobs and taxes. He is also looking for spending cuts in a number of federal programs. While many businesses have cheered his broader agenda, the uncertainty over tariffs on close trading partners Mexico and Canada has rattled markets. He has proposed and pulled back tariffs multiple times in just the past few months.

The president's moves are testing the resilience of the American economy, which has had robust growth and low unemployment. There are some early signs of strain, with a drop in consumer spending and fears about inflation weighing on businesses.

The stock market rallied in late 2024, fueled by investor enthusiasm about his agenda. But it has sputtered in recent weeks. The Dow Jones Industrial Average, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq Composite Index all closed Friday at lower levels than where they were when Trump was inaugurated on Jan. 20.

In a sign of the stop-and-start implementation of his trade agenda, the Trump administration last week paused 25% tariffs on most cars and parts that flow across the borders from Mexico and Canada less than 48 hours after they took effect.

In the interview that aired Sunday, the president demurred when asked if there were reassurances he could provide to businesses seeking more clarity on tariffs.

"Well, I think so," he said. He then added: "You know, the tariffs could go up as time goes by."

The president, who regularly bragged about the stock-market performance in his first term, played down its importance to his second-term agenda.

"Look, what I have to do is build a strong country. You can't really watch the stock market. If you look at China, they have a 100-year perspective," he said.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent last week also said that the U.S. economy could be in for a rough patch during this economic transition.

"The market and the economy have just become hooked," Bessent said on CNBC's "Squawk Box." "We've become addicted to this government spending, and there's going to be a detox period."

Trump has in recent days acknowledged the choppiness tariffs have brought. "There could be some disturbance, a little bit of disturbance," he said Friday. In his Tuesday speech to Congress, Trump defended his agenda while also noting there could be some disruptions.

The U.S. continued to generate jobs at a steady pace in February, according to data released last week. But economists see signs that employment could weaken in coming months, because of government layoffs, reduced government funding, uncertainty over tariffs and immigration restrictions. A sharply divided Congress also faces a Friday deadline to approve more funding to avoid a government shutdown, adding another wrinkle to the economic outlook.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick ruled out an economic contraction in his own television appearance Sunday.

"There's going to be no recession in America," he said on "Meet the Press" on NBC News. The U.S. economy hasn't been in a recession since the beginning of the pandemic in 2020.

Write to Tim Hanrahan at tim.hanrahan@wsj.com

data.title: Malaysian Shares Edge Lower, May See Bargain Hunting — Market Talk
data.description: Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index is 0.1% lower at 1546.34. Bargain hunting may follow Wall Street Friday's rebound, but gains could be short-lived due to profit-taking and geopolitical risks, Apex Securities says in a note. A key focus this week is U.S. inflation data due Wednesday for Fed policy signals, the brokerage says. It maintains a defensive stance, favoring REITs and selective property, consumer, and financial stocks with low foreign trade risks exposure. Among decliners, Tenaga Nasional and IHH Healthcare are down 0.7% each. Meanwhile, CelcomDigi adds 1.2% and Capital A is up 6.7%.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Japan Bank Lending Up 3.1% in February
data.description:
Japan's banks and other lenders held combined loans outstanding of 635.469 trillion yen in February, up 3.1% on year, reported the Bank of Japan in a preliminary report on Monday.

Major and regional banks held 557.4 trillion yen of loans, up 3.4% on year, according to central bank figures.

Japan's shinkin lenders, a cooperative bank system serving small and medium-sized enterprises and local residents, held more than 780 billion yen of loans in February, up a modest 0.9% on year.

In general, bank lending is considered one indicator of the economic health of a nation or region.

Bank loans are used to expand or facilitate business operations or acquire properties, or fund consumer outlays. Rising bank loan totals indicate not only demand, but a willingness of banks to extend credit.

Japan's financial institutions held 1,047.2 trillion yen of deposits and certificates of deposit in February, up 1.4% on year, added the Bank of Japan.

data.title: Methanex Corp Says 1.8 Million Tonne Methanol Plant, Geismar 3 (G3), In Geismar, Louisiana Was Impacted By An Unplanned Outage In Late February
data.description:
Methanex Corp :

  • METHANEX CORP - 1.8 MILLION TONNE METHANOL PLANT, GEISMAR 3 (G3), IN GEISMAR, LOUISIANA WAS IMPACTED BY AN UNPLANNED OUTAGE IN LATE FEBRUARY

  • METHANEX CORP - MANAGEMENT CURRENTLY ESTIMATES A PLANT STARTUP BY EARLY MAY 2025.

  • METHANEX CORP - THIS OUTAGE IS EXPECTED TO PRIMARILY IMPACT Q2 FINANCIAL RESULTS DUE TO LOWER PRODUCED METHANOL SALES

data.title: Seven & I: Remain Focused on Identifying All Other Avenues to Continue Driving Shareholder Value
Koonenberry Gold Says Newmont Unit Starts Drilling at New South Wales Copper-Gold Project Joint Venture
data.description:
Koonenberry Gold said that Newmont Exploration, an indirect wholly-owned subsidiary of Newmont , started a generative air core drilling program at selected targets across the Fairholme copper-gold joint venture project in New South Wales, according to a Monday Australian bourse filing.

The drilling program is fully funded by Newmont, with exploration expenditure of around AU$1.1 million spent by the end of January. Drilling will test several geophysical targets of up to 10 holes, the filing said.

Koonenberry Gold holds a 51% interest in the project, while Newmont is in the earn-in phase.

Newmont has the right to earn up to an 80% interest by funding AU$5 million of expenditure costs and carry Koonenberry up to AU$15 million of expenditures at its discretion.

Koonenberry Gold's shares were up 3% in recent Monday trade, while Newmont's shares gained about 1%.

data.title: Japan Logs Current Account Deficit in January
data.description:
Japan reported a current account deficit of 257.6 billion yen in January, from a surplus of 334.3 billion yen a year earlier, preliminary data released by the Ministry of Finance on Monday showed.

That figure was higher than the consensus estimate for a surplus of 230.5 billion yen but lower than the 1.077 trillion yen surplus in December 2024.

Exports grew year over year to 7.502 trillion yen from 7.35 trillion yen, and imports climbed to 10.44 trillion yen from 8.873 trillion yen a year before, the report said.

The goods account deficit widened to 2.938 trillion yen from 1.523 trillion yen in the prior year, and the primary income surplus ballooned to 3.602 trillion yen from 2.988 trillion yen, the report said.

data.title: The High-Pressure Tactics Gloria Allred Uses — On Her Own Clients — WSJ
data.description:
By Khadeeja Safdar

Gloria Allred invited the media to join a video call in March 2021 to hear one of her clients, Efrosina Angelova, share allegations of sexual and physical abuse against actor Armie Hammer.

Angelova cried as she read her statement describing a 2017 incident in which she accused the Hollywood star of raping her when she was 20 years old, slamming her against a wall and beating her feet. She said she "thought that he was going to kill me."

After the press conference, Allred gave interviews about the case where she burnished her brand as a champion of women. A month later, Angelova told her famous lawyer she was suffering panic attacks.

"Get a hold of yourself," Allred said, in a recording of the call. "How old are you? Get a hold of yourself." The lawyer reminded Angelova she was giving her "tough love" and that she wasn't paying her $1,200 hourly fee.

Allred's lifelong crusade against sexual misconduct, representing women accusing Jeffrey Epstein, Bill Cosby, Harvey Weinstein, R. Kelly, Sean Combs and others of misdeeds, has made her a leading figure in the #MeToo movement. Even at age 83, Allred, along with her former law school classmates Michael Maroko and Nathan Goldberg, runs the go-to law firm for women's rights cases in the U.S. Some women turn to Allred before they call the police.

For decades, Allred has hugged and comforted her clients while they give tearful accounts of their alleged abuse at press conferences. But that public image is often at odds with the lawyer's behavior behind the scenes. There, clients said, Allred and her firm ignored their wishes and pushed them to agree to confidential settlements.

Some clients said they were shocked at the disconnect between Allred's image as an advocate who empowers women and gives them choices and her private approach. They said they contacted her firm at the worst moments of their lives, when they felt vulnerable and alone. They now question whether retaining the firm was in their best interest, both emotionally and financially, and whether decisions were made primarily to benefit Allred and her firm.

Few details are public about what happens between Allred and her clients, mostly women, without the cameras. The reason: Clients are told to keep discussions secret and are required to sign agreements that bar them from suing the firm in court or publicizing disputes with their lawyers.

This account is based on audio recordings, documents and interviews with more than four dozen people, including women who have approached Allred for her services, former clients who have retained her firm, people in whom clients confided during their experiences and lawyers who have worked both alongside and against the firm.

The people said that while Allred consoles women on camera, in private she scolds and intimidates them and threatens to drop them as clients if they disobey her. Some said the firm's lawyers repeatedly conveyed the idea that the women were lucky to be talking to them. And even though Allred publicly says she wants women to speak out about injustice, some were told to delete text and video evidence, and several said her firm pressured them to sign nondisclosure agreements that protect predators.

"Everything that I wanted, I was shushed. My lawyers were not having any of what I said," said Ashley Kostial, who retained Allred's firm after she reported a co-worker for raping her. "When I heard Gloria Allred, very naively, like most women, I was very excited."

She said Allred's law partner, Goldberg, who helped negotiate a settlement with Kostial's employer in 2020, told her she was lucky because most women don't get any money. She said she was pressured to sign an NDA. "I was given the impression that if it didn't get done quickly," Kostial said, "I was going to be f — ed very astronomically."

Allred's firm negotiated a $300,000 settlement and an NDA for Caroline Wallner in 2021 after she accused author Neil Gaiman of sexually exploiting her, at the time believing her case was isolated. But when other Gaiman accusers came forward last year, Wallner alleged legal malpractice.

A lawyer at Allred's firm told Wallner this January she couldn't sue the firm or publicize her experience with her former lawyer, citing clauses in their retainer agreement. The provisions force unhappy clients to bring grievances to confidential arbitration — something Allred has publicly criticized. On its website, Allred, Maroko & Goldberg says forced arbitration "deprives survivors an opportunity to be heard and seek justice in a system of checks and balances."

The lawyer also made clear Allred's firm had recently obtained — from the opposing counsel — what it called "sexually provocative" texts, photos and videos Wallner sent to Gaiman. Wallner said the move was an attempt to "victim shame" her. "I had this feeling all along that they were never truly on my side, and I thought I was just being paranoid," she said.

More than a dozen women described similar interactions with the law firm, which included having their requests dismissed, being limited from speaking about their experiences — publicly but also with family, other victims or therapists — or being made to fear interactions with the media without Allred. Some questioned whether their payouts were as high as they could be, or whether they received justice through the process.

Angelova, the woman who was told to "get a hold of yourself," said she felt betrayed when Allred discussed her case in a documentary about Hammer, even though Angelova repeatedly told her she didn't want to be included. "I still can't fathom it," she said. "Imagine her scheduling the interview, putting on makeup, sitting down, looking at the camera and giving them that interview — all without telling me."

Allred said her clients aren't forced to sign confidentiality settlements and mediators are often present to ensure the client's decision is voluntary. She said she requires disputes with clients be handled in private arbitration to protect them from violating confidential settlements. Clients can always speak with therapists about what the accused said or did to them, she said, though there are sometimes "legal reasons" to advise them not to discuss communications with their lawyers.

Allred and her partners declined to comment on specific complaints in this article made by clients or former clients, citing legal and ethical duties. They said their lack of responses are "not an admission that we agree with the allegations made in your questions. To the contrary, we do not."

"We have represented approximately 10,000 clients in our almost 50 years of practice, the vast majority of whom have been very happy with their outcome and our legal representation," Allred wrote in a statement. "To criticize me and my law firm based on interviews of a mere handful of those 10,000 clients is misleading and wrong."

Gaiman and Hammer have both denied the allegations of abuse. Andrew Brettler, a lawyer for both men, didn't respond to requests for comment. Los Angeles prosecutors in May 2023 said they wouldn't pursue criminal charges against Hammer.

Secret agreements

Some of the practices employed by Allred's firm, including retainer agreements that force private arbitration in case of disputes and promise only to pursue confidential settlements, are employed by other firms that represent clients in abuse cases. But not many attorneys have achieved Allred's status as an advocate who fights for women at all costs.

NDAs have been criticized in the wake of the #MeToo movement for allowing serial predators, including Harvey Weinstein, to keep women with similar accusations from knowing about each other. Allred helped negotiate a $125,000 settlement with Weinstein for a client in 2004, long before dozens of women came forward about his sexual misconduct, according to the New York Times in 2019. Weinstein was convicted of sexual crimes including rape and is incarcerated.

NDAs curtailing people's rights to report a crime aren't enforceable, and many states, including New York, have restricted the use of confidentiality provisions for sexual-harassment claims.

Allred's firm has continued to negotiate settlements of sexual-abuse claims with NDAs, saying some women choose to keep their trauma and payouts private.

Some clients and lawyers familiar with the matter said the conditions of the deals negotiated by Allred, which effectively restrict what victims can say to spouses or therapists, are particularly onerous.

Allred's firm negotiated a settlement with an NDA for musician and composer Nomi Abadi in July 2018 with Danny Elfman, a Hollywood composer, over her allegations of sexual misconduct. Elfman has denied Abadi's sexual misconduct allegations.

Abadi and Elfman weren't allowed to have copies of the agreement, to ensure it wasn't disclosed to anyone, according to an email and people familiar with the matter.

Maroko, her lawyer, also took her only copy of a police report she had filed about Elfman, and later told her to delete text messages from Elfman, the people said and emails show.

When Abadi engaged a different law firm to sue Elfman over payments she said he hadn't made, the police report wasn't in her file from Allred's firm.

Allred negotiated a $1.25 million settlement for Olympic gold medalist McKayla Maroney in a 2016 deal with USA Gymnastics about the sexual abuse she suffered as a minor from Larry Nassar.

But Maroney said she was "forced to agree to a nondisparagement clause and confidentiality provision" that said she would have to pay more than $100,000 if she spoke of the settlement or the abuse by Nassar, according to a 2017 lawsuit against USA Gymnastics.

After allegations about Nassar were more widely known, Maroney filed the suit with a new attorney, arguing the confidentiality agreement negotiated by Allred's firm was illegal under California law. She later received a settlement totaling more than $6 million with no confidentiality provision from parties including the U.S. Olympic & Paralympic Committee, according to people familiar with the matter.

Nassar was convicted of crimes including the sexual abuse of children and is incarcerated. USA Gymnastics didn't respond to requests for comment.

Several women said they expected Allred to applaud them for their courage in coming forward and were taken aback when she said they had hurt their chances of getting paid in a settlement by reporting or publicizing their allegations.

Michelle Rhoades alleged Axl Rose, the lead singer of rock band Guns N' Roses, raped her when she was a teenager in the 1980s. She said she told Allred and Goldberg about the alleged abuse during a consultation in July 2019 after posting about the incident on her Facebook page. "She told me she could have gotten me hush money, if it were not for the Facebook post," Rhoades said. "That's the opposite of what I wanted."

A lawyer for Rose didn't respond to requests for comment.

Secrecy was also expected from prospective clients. Allred "said not to tell anyone that I talked to them," Rhoades said. Actual clients said they were routinely told not to talk to family about interactions with Allred and not to take notes during discussions with the firm's lawyers.

'Don't be a fool'

Wallner approached Allred's firm about her experience with Gaiman in December 2021. She had worked as a caretaker at Gaiman's Woodstock, N.Y., property and accused the author of coercing her into sex acts by making her fear that she and her three daughters would lose their place to live during the Covid pandemic if she didn't comply.

The settlement agreement — signed within a day of retaining the firm — prevents Wallner from suing Gaiman or speaking about her allegations against him to family, friends and associates, and doesn't note an exception for a therapist. It also requires Wallner and Gaiman to destroy photos, texts and videos and prohibits Wallner from reporting the allegations to "any court, governmental agency, or before any tribunal whatsoever or wheresoever." If Wallner is asked to make disclosures by a "valid legal process," she is required to give Gaiman notice.

Last month, Gaiman was sued by a former nanny, who said in her lawsuit the sexual abuse started in February 2022 — about six weeks after Wallner signed her NDA. Gaiman has denied the allegations and said the relationship was consensual.

After learning of other Gaiman accusers last year on a Tortoise Media podcast, Wallner said she wrote Goldberg, her lawyer at Allred's firm, to ask if she could speak publicly or support the other women.

Goldberg told her he would no longer represent her if she broke her NDA, she said. "He said don't be a fool, consider yourself one of the lucky ones, because most women don't get any money," she said.

Wallner said she shared her experience anyway. Wallner and Gaiman have filed arbitration claims alleging the other has violated the NDA.

Wallner recently hired a different lawyer to send a letter to Allred's firm alleging malpractice. She said the firm hadn't done enough to earn its fees and that it failed to fully research her claims, convincing her the experience was a one-off and that Gaiman wasn't a predator. Goldberg told her the $300,000 settlement was the best she could do, she said, and that Gaiman couldn't bad-mouth her with the NDA. She said at the time she felt shame for what happened with Gaiman, thinking her case was isolated.

In a letter this January, John West, a lawyer at Allred's firm, wrote that Wallner couldn't speak out or sue because of her retainer agreement, which requires arbitration.

He also wrote that the firm reached out to Gaiman's lawyer, Brettler, after receiving her legal threat and learned of the "sexually provocative" material she sent Gaiman that he said helped show she didn't have a case against the the law firm. West sent Wallner what he called a "sampling" of this material from Brettler. Wallner said she took West's action as an effort to silence her.

"I didn't send any actual nude photos or videos to Neil, but imagine if I had," Wallner said. "To think that this is Gloria Allred's firm that is supposed to care about women."

Public humiliation

Some people said Allred's team emphasized the shame and humiliation that could come from going public to push clients to sign NDAs quickly, even when some said they were prepared to be public.

Maroko told Abadi, who had earlier taken the matter to the Los Angeles police, to get a settlement done because associates of Elfman would be willing to smear her if she didn't settle the matter quickly and quietly, people familiar with the matter said.

Kostial said that even though Goldberg and her local attorney threatened litigation in a demand letter to SAP, her employer, her retainer agreement with the lawyers said her legal representation would be terminated if a settlement wasn't reached.

Kostial, who is gay, said the lawyers told her SAP would review her sexual history if the matter wasn't settled, and it was the company's belief she wasn't only interested in women. She had prepared her family to go public, but that wasn't presented as an option. "I was constantly told I don't want to [go to litigation] and that this is best for me," she said.

She said much of the day spent mediating the settlement involved going back and forth on the dollar amount. She was asked to read the eight-page settlement document in the room with lawyers and sign on the same day, she said. The lawyers were entitled to collect 40%, or about $100,000, of the amount.

Kostial's NDA states that if she is asked about the status of her allegations, she can only say "I can't discuss it" or "I would prefer not to discuss this matter." The clause, she said, hurt her career prospects by making it impossible to explain the gap in her résumé at job interviews and made her feel isolated because she couldn't share what she was going through with friends or family.

"They told me that everything was boilerplate," she said. Other lawyers she engaged later to review the deal told her she could have negotiated those elements and that it is unusual to sign an NDA within a day.

SAP declined to comment.

Several other women said Allred and her team didn't present a lawsuit as an option to them. The firm specified in retainer agreements reviewed by the Journal that it would only represent the clients for settlement talks, not a lawsuit. If a client didn't sign a settlement or walked away, she could still be responsible for some costs, according to the retainers reviewed by the Journal.

Allred didn't address the specific cases but said her firm collects evidence during settlement negotiations from the accused to better advise clients whether a confidential settlement would be a better outcome than litigation. She said her firm takes pride in resolving cases quickly since many of its clients can't afford to wait months or years.

She also said her firm takes on many cases pro bono and clients aren't responsible for costs if the firm is unable to reach a settlement. If a client terminates her firm and a case is subsequently settled, Allred said her firm, like other law firms, can claim payments for some costs.

Allred said some of her clients retain her firm for negotiations only and don't wish to have the case litigated to protect their privacy. If the negotiations don't result in a settlement, the firm will sometimes offer a new retainer to represent them in a lawsuit. "It depends on the facts, the law, and the client," she said.

Media deals

Allred's personal story has helped lay the foundation for her image as a feminist who fights for women. In her autobiography, she said she was raped at gunpoint while on a trip to Mexico when she was in her 20s. She wrote she didn't report the incident because she didn't think the police would believe her and returned to California where she said she got an illegal abortion. The experience inspired her, a single mother, to go to law school and fight for women's rights.

With Maroko and Goldberg, she started a firm in 1976 that she said has since represented thousands of victims of sexual abuse, discrimination or harassment. Allred's career was lauded in a 2018 Netflix documentary called "Seeing Allred" that shows her as an advocate who helps empower victims of sexual misconduct and discrimination to speak out.

Allred has long utilized press conferences and tabloids to bring attention to herself and her clients, even taking the woman known as Jane Roe from the landmark Roe v. Wade abortion case on a media tour after the 1973 Supreme Court decision. Allred didn't argue that case; two lawyers in Texas did.

Some women who appeared with Allred at her press conferences said she didn't represent them in any settlement or lawsuit and that they later felt their experiences were being used to help Allred lure new clients who were better candidates for NDAs.

Allred also coordinated encounters with tabloids, a few said, and brought clients offers to sell their stories or photos. A "story rights" agreement signed by former client Rachel Uchitel in 2009 retained Allred to negotiate media contracts about her life story for a 20% cut.

Uchitel, who was at the center of a tabloid frenzy in 2009 over her relationship with Tiger Woods, said she hired Allred to protect her. Allred tried to negotiate paid media deals, which Uchitel initially declined.

Allred invited the media to a press conference with Uchitel but quickly canceled when Woods's lawyers contacted the firm. She instead helped Uchitel negotiate an $8 million settlement and NDA with Woods.

"I feel like I helped create a monster," said Uchitel, who now regrets both the NDA and hiring Allred. Uchitel said Allred told her the firm could negotiate paid media deals that didn't break her NDA, which led her to believe she could do paid media on her own if she didn't reference Woods.

But in 2011, Allred pressed Uchitel to give up $3 million of her settlement when Woods's lawyers accused Uchitel of violating her NDA, which Uchitel maintained she hadn't done. Uchitel said Allred warned her she would drop her as a client if she didn't concede the sum, but Allred negotiated with Woods's lawyers to keep all of her own legal fees. Uchitel took Allred's firm to arbitration and was awarded $600,000 in 2014.

Allred said media contracts account "for less than one-tenth of one percent of the income" of her legal practice, and they are primarily an accommodation for clients who have asked for her representation on these matters. She said she doesn't charge her clients for legal advice regarding speaking to the press.

'The moneyshot'

In January 2021, Angelova posted messages on her Instagram page she had received from Hammer during the course of their relationship. The messages, which discussed rape and cannibalism, went viral online. Other women also stepped forward with allegations against the Hollywood star.

Angelova reached out to Allred and signed a retainer agreement that stated Allred would help Angelova pro bono to provide evidence to law enforcement and act as Angelova's media spokesperson "when, as and if needed." It didn't include settlement negotiations or filing a lawsuit.

The agreement also had the clauses that compelled Angelova into private arbitration if she had a dispute with Allred. She asked Allred's firm to strike the language, but the firm declined to take her on if she didn't agree to the stipulations. Angelova eventually signed the agreement.

Angelova, who had already spoken with the Los Angeles Times about her allegations, wanted the reporter to scale back some graphic details of the alleged rape in a forthcoming article, but Allred advised her to pull out completely, which she did.

Allred helped her file a police report and told her not to speak publicly during the criminal investigation, offering to talk on her behalf.

When Angelova told her she wanted to speak out, Allred listed options, including television and print interviews that would include Allred, a potential movie, a book deal, paid interviews and licensing photos. "That's what we call the moneyshot," Allred said, according to a recording, referring to photos of women with their accused.

"This is the kind of conversation that anybody who is smart and experienced in this town would have with A-list celebrities," Allred said.

Angelova told Allred she didn't want to profit from her story.

"I've never had a man say, oh no, don't get me any money," Allred replied. "Women are socialized to think there is something wrong with that."

Allred steered her toward a press conference, arguing it was important for the public to see Angelova. She appeared on video from Bulgaria, her home country, and tearfully read her statement.

Not long after, a producer approached Angelova to interview her for a documentary about the Hammer family. Angelova said she declined and told Allred to send a letter to make sure she wasn't included.

"I cannot express enough how much I don't want any part of my trauma to be lumped in a documentary" about them, she wrote in an August 2021 email to Allred, part of a series of emails reviewed by the Journal. "Please let me know what we can do about this because it's incredibly triggering and I feel helpless."

Allred replied a month later, declining to help. She wrote in an email she hadn't been retained for that purpose.

A year later, after a trailer for the documentary was released, Angelova said she was shocked to learn Allred would appear in the documentary discussing her case.

In an Aug. 20, 2022, email, Allred wrote to Angelova, "Effie, if I did an interview with them, it was a long time ago and I do not even remember it."

Angelova said she felt so helpless she wanted to turn to Brettler, the attorney who worked for Hammer, to help stop the documentary. Angelova said she believed at the time that Brettler was no longer representing Hammer, and emailed him.

Allred said she would resign as her attorney if she spoke to him by herself. "When I resign, I will also notify law enforcement, that I am no longer representing you in the criminal case. If media contacts me, I will also inform that that I am no longer representing you. Once I resign, please do not ask me to represent you later. Under no circumstances will I change my mind. I will not be angry with you, Effie. Feel free to make whatever decision you think is best for you," Allred wrote in an Aug. 26 email.

Angelova said she felt Allred was threatening her that being dropped would harm Angelova's reputation and hurt the criminal investigation into Hammer, which was still active at that time.

When a Los Angeles Times reporter approached Angelova, she told the reporter Allred had participated in the documentary without her knowledge. The newspaper sought comment from Allred.

Allred sent an email to Angelova to tell her that she did have permission to conduct the interview, citing the retainer agreement.

"This is an urgent matter," Allred wrote in the Sept. 2 email to Angelova. "A simple 'yes' that I was authorized in my role as your lawyer and spokesperson to do the interview in question will suffice."

When Angelova didn't reply, Allred's assistant sent her a string of text messages, repeatedly telling her it was "urgent." Angelova replied to Allred: "I can't retrospectively 'authorize' something that was done a long time ago that I never knew was happening."

"The clause in the retainer says 'if needed,' Angelova told the Journal. "I felt it was not needed for her to do that interview or any of the other ones." The retainer didn't specify who would decide which interviews were needed.

In a Sept. 6, 2022, letter, Allred resigned as Angelova's attorney, saying Angelova had repeatedly communicated with Brettler and made comments to the Los Angeles Times without informing her.

Angelova said the experience with Allred has worsened her trauma. "I didn't realize it at the time," she said, "but now I feel she was just using me as a commercial."

Write to Khadeeja Safdar at khadeeja.safdar@wsj.com

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Sunday at 9 PM ET
data.description:
U.S. stock futures fall after a rough week on Wall Street, as Trump won't rule out recession

U.S. stock futures fell Sunday, as President Donald Trump offered little reassurance after the S&P 500 had its worst week since September.

Trump Declines to Rule Out Recession

In an interview that aired Sunday, the president demurred when asked if there were reassurances he could provide to businesses seeking more clarity on tariffs.

Tariff Wars Are Often Short. Their Legacies Aren't.

Economists fear that Trump's tariffs, once they take hold, could have unexpected effects long after he leaves office.

Markets Finally Woke Up to Tariff Reality. Is This a Buying Opportunity?

Your correspondent remains torn, but if you believe in the Trump pitch, now might be a time to take a swing.

China Struggles to Shake Off Disinflationary Pressure as Trade Threats Loom

Consumer prices nosedived in February, ending a year of tepid price growth.

America's job market is eerily similar to the 1990s dot-com bubble - and, yes, it's a worry

March 20 marks the 25th anniversary of the dot-com bust. Could it happen again?

GOP Government Funding Plan Sets Up Clash With Democrats

The measure to avoid a government shutdown next weekend boosts military funding, cuts nondefense spending.

Stock Investors Go on Defense With Dividends

Some investors are sheltering from market swings in shares of companies with high payouts.

A Government Shutdown Could Create Short-Term Volatility. But It's Mostly Noise.

Broadcom and Marvell shares have been hit recently. But Nvidia alone is responsible for about three quarters of the chip sector's lost market cap in the weeks since the DeepSeek news.

The last seven days marked the third "brutal" week in a row for bank stocks.

Consumer Staples Are Crushing the Market. Why They Aren't the Stocks to Buy.

It's time to look elsewhere-unless you're betting on a recession.

Bally's Offers Financing Deal to Star
data.description:
By Mike Cherney

SYDNEY--American betting and entertainment company Bally's has offered to provide embattled Australian casino operator Star with additional financing, in a deal that could help Bally's expand its international footprint.

Bally's would underwrite a capital raise of at least 250 million Australian dollars, equivalent to US$157.7 million, which would involve the issuance of convertible notes that could be changed into at least 50.1% of Star shares. Bally's would support allowing existing Star shareholders to participate in a significant portion of the offering on a pro rata basis.

Star, which is trying to stay in business after facing regulatory scrutiny and difficult trading conditions, said it would review the proposal from Bally's, but said there is no certainty it will be progressed.

Write to Mike Cherney at mike.cherney@wsj.com

data.title: Sovereign Metals' Testwork on Malawi Project Coarse Flake Graphite Returns Higher-than-Target Purity Rate
data.description:
Sovereign Metals said purification test work on Kasiya coarse flake returned a rate higher than the target, reinforcing the potential benefits of the material for downstream customers, according to a Monday filing with the Australian bourse.

Purification of the coarse flake graphite achieved 99.95% loss of ignition (LOI) purity using acid purification and 99.98% LOI purity via alkaline purification, compared with the target of >99.0%.

The results indicate the potential to cut reagent consumption and waste generation in the production of high-purity flake or targeted high-end applications, the filing stated.

data.title: Woodside Energy, Baker Hughes Team Up to Develop Lower Carbon Power Generation Technology Platform
data.description:
Woodside Energy Group and Nasdaq-listed Baker Hughes signed a technology development agreement to work on a lower carbon power generation platform designed for oil and gas, heavy industries, and other smaller scale applications, according to a March 6 joint statement.

The collaboration will use the patented Net Power platform to generate power and capture carbon dioxide emissions, per the statement.

Baker Hughes and Woodside are also looking into adding other development partners to the program, as well as exploring the industrial market scalability of Net Power's platform.

Financial terms were not disclosed.

Woodside Energy's shares were up nearly 2% in recent Monday trade.

data.title: GOP Government Funding Plan Sets Up Clash With Democrats — WSJ
data.description:
By Katy Stech Ferek

WASHINGTON — Republicans are charging ahead with a proposal endorsed by President Trump that would keep the government funded into the fall while reducing spending, setting up a clash with Democrats already steaming over sharp cuts to federal jobs and programs.

The proposal, if it clears a vote in the House as soon as Tuesday and then the Senate, would avoid a government shutdown next weekend and keep federal agencies funded through Sept. 30.

The House measure lowers the amount of nondefense discretionary spending by $13 billion from the 2024 funding level, while increasing the amount of money for defense spending by about $6 billion. It also boosts funding for border enforcement. The military funding is meant to win over defense hawks who have rejected past short-term measures that they said have underfunded the U.S. military.

Party leaders have expressed confidence that they will be able to pass the measure in the House, despite intraparty fighting that has forced them to rely heavily on Democrats on past funding votes, a regular embarrassment for party chiefs. With House Democrats appearing largely united in opposition, saying the measure cedes too much power over spending to the White House, Republicans will need almost every GOP lawmaker to stay in line, due to their narrow 218-214 majority.

Representatives for House Republican leaders said that they worked closely with Trump in crafting the bill, and House Speaker Mike Johnson (R., La.) said that he has enough votes to pass the measure.

Trump urged Republicans to back the proposal, termed a continuing resolution or CR. He said on Truth Social that the GOP has "put together, under the circumstances, a very good funding bill." He added: "NO DISSENT."

Funding for federal agencies runs out on Friday night. With no new legislation, many federal employees would be furloughed, while others would continue to report to work with no pay.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D., N.Y.), who opposes the GOP bill, has said he didn't think that Republicans would be able to come up with enough votes for it to pass. In a statement Saturday with other House Democratic leaders, he said the measure "does nothing to protect Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid, while exposing the American people to further pain throughout this fiscal year."

More than a dozen House Republicans have never voted to pass a temporary funding bill under Johnson's leadership, thanks largely to unmet demands for more spending cuts. But many of these GOP holdouts are expected to get on board this time, thanks to the pressure from Trump and ambitions for passing a multitrillion-dollar Republican package pairing tax cuts and spending reductions later this year.

"There's overwhelming support for the bill," Rep. Chip Roy (R., Texas), a member of the conservative House Freedom Caucus, said on "Fox & Friends Weekend" on Fox News. "There are still a few folks who don't like it."

If House Republicans manage to stay united and pass the proposal over Democratic opposition, it will need at least 60 votes in the Senate, which has only 53 Republican lawmakers, to get to Trump's desk. Senate Democrats will need to decide if they want to get onboard the GOP bill, or risk getting blamed for the first shutdown since early 2019.

Sen. Elissa Slotkin (D., Mich.) said Sunday on NBC's "Meet the Press" that she will withhold her vote for the proposal until she sees "some assurances that whatever we pass next week is going to ensure that money is spent the way Congress intends."

Sen. Patty Murray (D., Wash.), the top Democrat on the chamber's appropriations committee, said that the GOP proposal, which was unveiled Saturday, would give Trump and Musk the power of the purse that is typically reserved for Congress.

She said Congress should pass a short-term measure to prevent a shutdown and finish work on bipartisan funding bills to "ensure our constituents have a say in how federal funding is spent." The overall amount of money for federal agencies falls below both the fiscal year 2024 funding level and the level set in 2023 in a compromise between former President Joe Biden and former House Speaker Kevin McCarthy (R., Calif.).

The last time Congress faced a shutdown, Trump, as president-elect, spoke out against a bipartisan deal that Johnson had negotiated with Democrats in December, prompting him to kill the measure before lawmakers voted on it. They later approved a slimmed-down version that set the March deadline.

Unlike the December measure, the 99-page proposal released on Saturday doesn't contain funding for pet projects requested by individual lawmakers for their hometowns, something that billionaire Trump cost-cutting czar Elon Musk had criticized.

Sen. Lindsey Graham (R., S.C.), said he wasn't a fan of the plan but didn't think the government would shut down. He said he wanted to know how Congress would strengthen defense and border security before voting on the measure.

Annual funding bills for federal agencies, when passed by Congress in previous years, come with lengthy reports — written jointly by Republicans and Democrats — with specific directives on how agency leaders should spend their allocated amount of federal money. The proposal from Johnson on Saturday contained far less guidance, giving Trump administration officials more power to spend the money as they wish.

Democratic leaders have said they want to extend government funding levels by a shorter period, giving lawmakers more time to negotiate a package of funding proposals that contain the bipartisan spending guidance for each agency.

Write to Katy Stech Ferek at katy.stech@wsj.com
